title: Diffs and patch review

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Diffs and patch review; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Diffs and patch review
sources.claim: A diff compares two explicitly named states, such as working tree to index, index to HEAD, or one commit to another, and reports line-oriented changes rather than intent.

sources.claim: A diff compares two explicitly named states, such as working tree to index, index to HEAD, or one commit to another, and reports line-oriented changes rather than intent.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A diff compares two explicitly named states, such as working tree to index, index to HEAD, or one commit to another, and reports line-oriented changes rather than intent.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: git diff reviews unstaged edits while git diff --staged reviews the snapshot proposed for the next commit. Your goal is to review a patch for unintended files, whitespace-only churn and behavior changes. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Selective staging and first commit; Working tree, index and HEAD; Log, show and graph. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A diff compares two explicitly named states, such as working tree to index, index to HEAD, or one commit to another, and reports line-oriented changes rather than intent. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Renames are inferred, binary changes are limited, generated formatting can obscure logic, and an empty diff can mean the wrong pair of states was compared. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: One diff command always shows every change in the repository, including staged, unstaged, and untracked content.

includes: Read line-level changes in working, staged and historical comparisons.
Review a patch for unintended files, whitespace-only churn and behavior changes.
excludes: Renames are inferred, binary changes are limited, generated formatting can obscure logic, and an empty diff can mean the wrong pair of states was compared.

outcomes: Review a patch for unintended files, whitespace-only churn and behavior changes.

examples.title: Diffs and patch review: direct case
examples.situation: git diff reviews unstaged edits while git diff --staged reviews the snapshot proposed for the next commit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: git diff reviews unstaged edits while git diff --staged reviews the snapshot proposed for the next commit.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Name or understand both comparison endpoints and inspect untracked status separately so no category of work is silently omitted.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Name or understand both comparison endpoints and inspect untracked status separately so no category of work is silently omitted.

examples.title: Diffs and patch review: boundary case
examples.situation: Comparing a release tag with the current branch isolates all committed changes since that release rather than local uncommitted edits.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Comparing a release tag with the current branch isolates all committed changes since that release rather than local uncommitted edits.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Renames are inferred, binary changes are limited, generated formatting can obscure logic, and an empty diff can mean the wrong pair of states was compared.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: One diff command always shows every change in the repository, including staged, unstaged, and untracked content.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Review a patch for unintended files, whitespace-only churn and behavior changes.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: One diff command always shows every change in the repository, including staged, unstaged, and untracked content.
misconceptions.correction: Name or understand both comparison endpoints and inspect untracked status separately so no category of work is silently omitted.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: One diff command always shows every change in the repository, including staged, unstaged, and untracked content.

analogy.example: A signed lab notebook can model Diffs and patch review: stable entries record why a change happened, named markers identify milestones, and comparisons expose differences.
usefulFor: Inspecting immutable records, messages, selected versions, differences, and milestone names
limitations: Git history is a directed graph with movable references, merges, and content-addressed objects, not a single bound notebook.
Renames are inferred, binary changes are limited, generated formatting can obscure logic, and an empty diff can mean the wrong pair of states was compared.

recap.summary: You should now be able to review a patch for unintended files, whitespace-only churn and behavior changes. Keep this boundary in view: Renames are inferred, binary changes are limited, generated formatting can obscure logic, and an empty diff can mean the wrong pair of states was compared.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Diffs and patch review without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: One diff command always shows every change in the repository, including staged, unstaged, and untracked content.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Comparing a release tag with the current branch isolates all committed changes since that release rather than local uncommitted edits.
